label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this question. The story is about an employer calculating how much money Julia, the governess, should get. He starts by saying they agreed on 30 rubles a month, but Julia says 40. He insists on 30 and then subtracts various days when she didn't work, like Sundays, holidays, days when the kids were sick, days she had a toothache, etc. Then he adds more deductions for breaking things and the kids getting into trouble. He keeps taking money away, and Julia just stays silent, even though he's clearly giving her less than she's owed. At the end, he gets angry when she says "merci" and realizes she's been treated poorly elsewhere. Then he gives her the full 80 rubles instead. The question is why the employer made all those criticisms. The choices are: A) She had neglected her duties. B) He wanted to pay her as little money as possible. C) He wanted to make her realize she was being cheated and protest. D) He wanted to make her feel miserable. So first, I need to see if the criticisms were genuine or not. The employer keeps subtracting things, but some of them seem like excuses to pay her less. For example, he mentions subtracting for Sundays when she only took walks, but maybe that's part of her job? Also, the deductions for the teacup, the torn jacket, stolen shoes—maybe those were not her fault. The key point here is that Julia didn't protest any of these deductions. The employer later says he was playing a cruel joke on her to teach her a lesson. He says, "I played a little joke on you, a cruel lesson, just to teach you..." So his intention was to show her how easy it is to be taken advantage of and to encourage her to stand up for herself. Therefore, he wasn't genuinely trying to account for her work but was using those criticisms as a way to underpay her, hoping she would protest. When she didn't, he felt guilty and gave her the full amount. Looking at the options, B says he wanted to pay her as little as possible. But the employer later gives her the full amount, so maybe he wasn't trying to keep it low permanently. The purpose was to teach her a lesson by pretending to underpay her. That aligns more with option C, where he wanted her to realize she was being cheated and protest. Option D says he wanted to make her feel miserable. While the employer does make her feel bad, the main reason is to teach her to stand up for herself, not just to make her sad. Therefore, the answer is C. The employer wanted to make her realize she was being cheated and to protest. The employer's intention was to show her the harshness of the world and the need to have "teeth and claws" by playing this cruel lesson. Julia didn't protest, so the employer then felt bad and gave her the money
